title: React Router v6+
summary: Routing, nested routes, outlet, loaders, actions, navigation hooks, and protected route patterns in React Router v6/v7.

questions.prompt: `<Outlet />` in a React Router layout component renders:
choices: The matched child route's element at that position in the layout
questions.explanation: Outlet is the 'slot' where child routes appear inside a layout component. Without Outlet, child routes are defined but never render. A layout component wraps shared UI (nav, sidebar) and places Outlet where the page content goes.

questions.prompt: How does a loader improve on data fetching in useEffect?
choices: Loaders run faster due to server-side execution
Loader fetches data before the component renders — no loading spinner needed in the component; the component always has its data when it first renders
questions.explanation: useEffect runs after render — so the component renders once without data (showing a loader), then re-renders with data. React Router's loader runs before navigation completes. The component receives data on its first render via useLoaderData() — eliminating the empty-state flash.

questions.prompt: What does `useParams()` return in a route with path `user/:id`?
choices: An object { id: '42' } where '42' is the matched segment value
questions.explanation: useParams() returns an object with all dynamic path segments as keys. For path `user/:id` at URL `/user/42`, it returns `{ id: '42' }`. Note: always a string — parse to number if needed with `Number(params.id)`.

questions.prompt: The difference between `<Link to='/page'>` and `window.location.href = '/page'` is:
choices: No difference — both navigate to the same URL
Link triggers client-side navigation (no page reload, React state preserved). window.location.href triggers a full browser page load (React app restarts, state lost)
Link requires a server; href is client-only
questions.explanation: React Router intercepts Link clicks to update the URL via the History API — no reload. JavaScript continues running, React state is preserved, only the matched route changes. `window.location.href` is a full navigation — the browser tears down and reloads the entire app.

questions.prompt: Why use `navigate('/dashboard', { replace: true })` after login?
choices: Replace is required for redirect URLs
Replaces the current history entry so the back button doesn't return to the login page
questions.explanation: Without replace: history is [/, /login, /dashboard]. Back button goes to /login — the user is already logged in, so they get bounced to /dashboard again (frustrating). With replace: history is [/, /dashboard]. Back goes to home. Always use replace after authentication redirects.

questions.prompt: An `index: true` route matches when:
choices: The parent path matches exactly — the index route is the default child when no other child segment matches
It's the first route in the array
questions.explanation: An index route renders inside its parent's Outlet when the parent path is matched exactly. Example: parent path is `/dashboard`, index route renders at `/dashboard`. Without an index, `/dashboard` shows the layout but Outlet is empty until a child is selected.

questions.prompt: Which hooks come from React Router v6?
questions.explanation: useParams, useNavigate, and useSearchParams are React Router hooks. useEffect is a core React hook unrelated to routing (though you might use it alongside routing hooks).

questions.prompt: When a loader throws `new Response('Not Found', { status: 404 })`, React Router:
choices: Renders the nearest errorElement and provides the response to useRouteError()
questions.explanation: Throwing a Response from a loader triggers the errorElement for that route (or the nearest ancestor errorElement). The RouteError boundary renders instead of the normal component tree. Use useRouteError() inside the error component to read the thrown value.

questions.prompt: A parent route renders `<Dashboard />` but Dashboard has no `<Outlet />`. Child routes are defined. What happens at `/dashboard/settings`?
choices: Dashboard renders normally but Settings is invisible — there's no Outlet slot for it
questions.explanation: Child routes need an Outlet in the parent to render. Without `<Outlet />` in Dashboard, React Router has nowhere to insert Settings. The URL changes to /dashboard/settings but the UI only shows 'Dashboard' — Settings is matched but never rendered. This is a common silent bug.

questions.prompt: Protecting a route with loader-based redirect (`throw redirect('/login')`) is better than a wrapper component because:
choices: The redirect happens before navigation completes — the protected component never renders, so no protected data flashes
questions.explanation: A wrapper ProtectedRoute component renders first, then redirects — the protected component briefly renders before redirect. A loader throws before navigation completes, so the component never renders at all. For data-sensitive pages, the loader approach is more secure.

questions.prompt: `useLocation()` returns an object. Which property contains the URL path?
questions.explanation: `useLocation()` returns `{ pathname, search, hash, state, key }`. `pathname` is the path segment (`/dashboard/settings`). `search` is the query string (`?tab=orders`). `state` is navigation state passed via navigate().

questions.prompt: `<NavLink>` differs from `<Link>` in that it:
choices: Provides an `isActive` boolean to style the link when it matches the current URL
questions.explanation: NavLink's `className` and `style` props receive `{ isActive, isPending }`. This lets you apply active styling without manually checking `useLocation().pathname`. Perfect for navigation menus where the current page link should look different.

questions.prompt: A catch-all route `path: '*'` renders when:
choices: No other route in the tree matches the current URL
questions.explanation: `path: '*'` matches any URL that no other route has matched — it's the 404 fallback. React Router evaluates routes in specificity order (not definition order) and `*` has the lowest specificity, so it only wins when nothing else matches.

questions.prompt: When a loader is running (data is being fetched), `useNavigation().state` returns:
questions.explanation: `useNavigation().state` has three values: `'idle'` (no navigation), `'loading'` (loader running), `'submitting'` (form/action running). Use it to show global loading indicators during route transitions.

questions.prompt: What does `useSearchParams()` return and how do you read `?tab=orders`?
questions.code:
const [searchParams, setSearchParams] = useSearchParams();
const tab = searchParams.get('tab');
choices: tab is 'orders' (string) — or null if not present
tab is the full search string '?tab=orders'
questions.explanation: useSearchParams returns a tuple: [URLSearchParams, setFn]. `.get('tab')` returns the string value of the `tab` param or null. To update: `setSearchParams({ tab: 'profile' })` — this updates the URL query string reactively without a page reload.

questions.prompt: Passing state through navigation: `navigate('/confirm', { state: { orderId: 42 } })`. How do you read orderId on /confirm?
questions.explanation: Navigation state is passed in-memory (not in the URL). useLocation() on the target page returns the current location object including `.state`. This is useful for passing data to a confirmation page without exposing it in the URL.

questions.prompt: Which patterns correctly protect routes that require authentication?
choices: A ProtectedRoute wrapper that renders <Navigate to='/login' /> if not authed
A loader that throws redirect('/login') if no auth token
Using useEffect in every protected component to check auth
Adding auth check in the root errorElement
questions.explanation: Wrapper component (A) and loader redirect (B) are both valid patterns. B is preferred (component never renders). useEffect-in-component (C) causes flashes and is error-prone. errorElement (D) handles errors, not auth checks.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of `createBrowserRouter` vs older `<BrowserRouter>`?
choices: createBrowserRouter enables Data Router features (loaders, actions, deferred data). BrowserRouter is the older v5-style component wrapper without these features
questions.explanation: React Router v6.4 introduced Data Routers (createBrowserRouter, createHashRouter). These unlock loaders, actions, fetchers, and deferred data. The older `<BrowserRouter>` doesn't support these features. New projects should use createBrowserRouter.

questions.prompt: Lazy loading a route component uses:
choices: React.lazy(() => import('./Dashboard')) combined with a Suspense boundary around the Outlet
questions.explanation: `React.lazy(() => import('./Dashboard'))` creates a lazily loaded component. Wrapping the Outlet (or the Router) in `<Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>` handles the loading state. This code-splits the route's bundle — Dashboard's JS is only downloaded when that route is first visited.

questions.prompt: A route with `path: 'user/:id/details/:section'` at URL `/user/42/details/bio`. What does useParams() return?
questions.explanation: useParams() returns a key-value object for every named segment (`:name`). For path `user/:id/details/:section` at `/user/42/details/bio`: `{ id: '42', section: 'bio' }`. Static segments (user, details) are not in the params object — only the dynamic `:segments`.

flashcards.back: Renders the matched child route inside a layout component. Without Outlet in the parent, child routes are matched but never displayed.
Loader runs before navigation completes — component always has data on first render. useEffect runs after render — causes loading state flash. Prefer loaders for route-level data.
Replaces current history entry. Use after login to prevent back button returning to login page.
useParams(): `{ id: '42' }` from path segments. useSearchParams(): URLSearchParams for `?key=val`. Both return strings.
`throw redirect('/login')` inside loader if unauthorized. Component never renders. More secure than wrapper component which renders briefly before redirect.
Renders inside parent Outlet when parent path matches exactly. `{ index: true, element: <Home /> }` renders at parent's base path.
Link = client-side navigation, no reload, React state preserved. window.location.href = full browser reload, React app restarts.
`className={({ isActive }) => isActive ? 'active' : ''}` — auto-applies active class when URL matches. No manual location check needed.

project.brief: Design the route configuration for a multi-role SaaS app with: 1) Public routes: `/`, `/login`, `/signup`. 2) Authenticated user routes: `/dashboard`, `/dashboard/orders`, `/dashboard/orders/:orderId`. 3) Admin-only routes: `/admin`, `/admin/users`. Specify: the full createBrowserRouter config, how auth is checked (loader or wrapper), how admin check differs from user check, which components use Outlet and where, and how order detail data is loaded. Also: what happens at /admin if a non-admin user navigates there?

checklist.text: Public root with Login and Signup as children, no auth check
AuthGuard loader: checks session, throws redirect('/login') if no auth
AdminGuard loader: checks role === 'admin', throws redirect('/dashboard') if not admin
Order detail route: loader fetches order by params.orderId, errorElement for 404
Outlet present in Dashboard (for orders) and Admin (for users)

hints: Nest authenticated routes under a layout route that has a loader with the auth check.
Admin routes are a second nested layer — another route with its own admin-check loader.
Dashboard component: `<DashNav /><Outlet />` — orders render inside the same layout.
Loader throw redirect returns a Response — React Router catches it and navigates automatically.
